title: Vulcan OmniPro 220 Welding System Owner's Manual
description: Cover page of the Owner's Manual and Safety Instructions for the Vulcan OmniPro 220 Multiprocess Welding System. The page includes product information, safety warnings, contact details, and instructions for handling the manual.
visible_text: Owner's Manual & Safety Instructions
VULCAN
OMNIPRO 220
MULTIPROCESS WELDING SYSTEM

title: Safety Information and Table of Contents
description: This page contains the table of contents for a welding machine manual and presents important safety information including warning symbols, definitions, and general safety guidelines. It emphasizes critical safety warnings and precautions operators must follow to prevent injury or death.
visible_text: Table of Contents
Safety
Specifications
Controls
MIG/Flux-Cored Wire Welding
TIG/Stick Welding
Welding Tips
Maintenance
Parts List and Diagram
Warranty
WARNING SYMBOLS AND DEFINITIONS
DANGER - will result in death or serious injury
WARNING - could result in death or serious injury
CAUTION - could result in minor or moderate injury
NOTICE CAUTION - Addresses practices not related to personal injury
Read all Safety warnings and instructions
Never leave the Welder unattended while energized

title: Fume Gas Safety and Arc Ray Protection
description: This manual page outlines safety hazards and protective measures for welding and plasma cutting operations, including inhalation risks from toxic fumes and arc ray exposure. It provides 6 control measures for fume safety and 6 protective requirements for arc ray safety.
visible_text: Fume and Gas Safety
INHALATION HAZARD: Welding and Plasma Cutting produce toxic fumes
Keep head out of fumes. Do not breathe exhaust fumes
Use enough ventilation, exhaust at arc, or both
Work in a confined area only if it is well-ventilated, or while wearing an air-supplied respirator
Have a recognized specialist in Industrial Hygiene or Environmental Services check the operation and air quality
Arc Ray Safety
ARC RAYS can injure eyes and burn skin
Wear ANSI-approved welding eye protection featuring at least a number 10 shade lens rating
Wear leather leggings, fire resistant shoes or boots during use
Wear an approved head covering to protect the head and neck
Wear an approved welding jacket or long sleeves to protect forearms
When welding/cutting overhead or in confined spaces, wear flame resistant ear plugs or ear muffs

title: Electrical and Fire Safety Guidelines for Welding
description: This manual page provides critical safety instructions for welding machine operation, covering electrical hazards and fire prevention. It details 10 electrical safety rules and 9 fire safety procedures to protect operators from shock, burns, and combustion hazards.
visible_text: ELECTRIC SHOCK can KILL
Turn off, disconnect power, and discharge electrode to ground before setting down torch/electrode holder and before service
Do not touch energized electrical parts
Connect to grounded, GFCI-protected power supply only
Do not use near water or damp objects
People with pacemakers should consult their physician(s) before use
THIS WELDING MACHINE MUST BE CONNECTED TO POWER SOURCE IN ACCORDANCE WITH APPLICABLE ELECTRICAL CODES
ARC AND SLAG can cause fire
Clear away or protect flammable objects
Keep ABC-type fire extinguisher near work area and know how to use it
Do not operate welders in atmospheres containing dangerously reactive or flammable liquids, gases, vapors, or dust
Do not dispose of hot slag in containers holding combustible materials
After welding, make a thorough examination for evidence of fire

title: Welder Use Care Maintenance Safety Instructions
description: This manual page provides essential safety guidelines for welder operation, maintenance procedures, and gas shielded welding cylinder safety. It covers pre-use checks, preventive measures, maintenance requirements, and specific hazard warnings for welding equipment.
visible_text: Do not use the welder if the switch does not turn it on and off
Disconnect the plug from the power source before making any adjustments
Prevent unintentional starting. Ensure the switch is in the off-position
Store idle welders out of the reach of children
Use the welder and accessories in accordance with these instructions
Do not use the welder for pipe thawing
Maintain welders. Check for misalignment or binding of moving parts
Have your welder serviced by a qualified repair person
Maintain labels and nameplates on the Welder
Cylinders can explode when damaged
Do not weld on a pressurized or closed cylinder
Keep cylinders away from any electrical circuits
Use only correct gas shielding equipment
Protect gas cylinders from heat, being struck, physical damage
SAVE THESE INSTRUCTIONS

title: Grounding, Cords, and Safety Symbology Guide
description: This manual page covers critical safety information about proper grounding connections for welding machines, guidelines for power cords and extension cords, and a comprehensive symbology reference chart. It emphasizes preventing electric shock and hazards through correct grounding wire installation and cord usage.
visible_text: WARNING: TO PREVENT ELECTRIC SHOCK AND DEATH FROM INCORRECT GROUNDING WIRE CONNECTION
The green wire inside the cord is connected to the grounding system in the Welder
Do not use an extension cord on this welder
Use only one of the supplied power cords for this welder or an identical replacement cord
Do not patch cords of any length together
Wire Feed (Speed)
Workpiece Ground Cable
Torch Cable
Electric Shock Hazard. Do not touch energized parts
Inhalation Hazard. Keep fumes out of lungs and use proper ventilation
Fire Hazard. Keep flammable materials away during welding
Arc Ray Hazard. Wear welding helmet with properly rated filter lens
Pacemaker Hazard. Welding processes may interfere with pacemakers

title: Welding Machine Specifications MIG TIG Stick
description: This page presents detailed specifications for three types of welding machines: MIG, TIG, and Stick. It includes power input requirements, current ranges, duty cycles, and weldable materials for both 120VAC/60Hz and 240VAC/60Hz models.
visible_text: MIG
Power Input: 120VAC/60Hz, 240VAC/60Hz
Welding Current Range: 30-140A, 30-220A
Weldable Materials: Mild Steel, Stainless Steel, Aluminum
TIG
Welding Current Range: 10A-125A, 10A-175A
Weldable Materials: Mild Steel, Stainless Steel, Chrome Moly
Stick
Welding Current Range: 10A-80A, 10A-175A
Maximum OCV: 86VDC
Wire Spool Capacity: Up to 12 lb spool
Rated Duty Cycles

title: MIG/Flux-Cored Wire Spool Installation Setup
description: This manual page provides step-by-step instructions for installing and setting up wire spools on a MIG/flux-cored wire welder. It covers power switch safety procedures, door latch operation, and proper 1-2 pound wire spool installation with detailed diagrams showing component placement and orientation.
visible_text: MIG/Flux-Cored Wire Welding
Read the ENTIRE IMPORTANT SAFETY INFORMATION section
Turn the Power Switch OFF and unplug the Welder before setup
Wire Spool Installation/Wire Setup
Pull up on the Door Latch, then open the Door
1-2 Pound Wire Spool Installation: Remove the Wingnut and Spacer
Place the new Wire Spool over the Spool Spindle
To prevent wire feed problems, set the Spool so that it will unwind clockwise
Replace the Spacer over the Spool Spindle and secure Spool in place with the Wingnut
If Wire Spool can spin freely, Wingnut is too loose
Remove the protective foam and cardboard from the Welder before setup
Spool Brake Pad
For technical questions, please call 1-800-444-3353

title: 10-12 Pound Wire Spool Installation Instructions
description: This manual page provides step-by-step instructions for installing a 10-12 pound wire spool on a welding machine. It includes detailed diagrams of spool components, assembly procedures, and maintenance information for the wire feed system.
visible_text: 10-12 Pound Wire Spool Installation
Remove the Wingnut and Spacer
Place the Spool Adapter over the Spool Spindle
Place the new Wire Spool over the Adapter
set the Spool so that it will unwind clockwise
Replace the Spacer over the Spool Spindle
Wire must unwind in this direction
Turn the Feed Tensioner knob counterclockwise
Spool Knob, Wingnut, Spacer
10-12 lb Wire Spool, Spool Brake Pad
Feed Tensioner, Idler Arm

title: Feed Roller Instructions and Setup Guide
description: This manual page provides step-by-step instructions for checking, adjusting, and replacing the feed roller on a welding machine. It includes diagrams showing different groove types (solid core V-groove and flux-cored knurled groove) and their corresponding wire sizes.
visible_text: Feed Roller Instructions
Check that the Feed Roller is correct for the type of wire being used
Unscrew the Feed Roller Knob counterclockwise
Remove the Feed Roller Knob to expose the Feed Roller
Flip or replace the Feed Roller as needed
Solid Core V-Groove
Flux-Cored Knurled Groove
0.025 groove
0.045 groove
Screw the Feed Roller Knob back into place to secure the Feed Roller
the number showing is the same as the wire diameter on the Spool
For technical questions, please call 1-800-444-3353

title: Wire Feed and Cable Connection Setup
description: This manual page provides detailed instructions for connecting the Gun Cable Connector and Wire Feed Control Cable to a welding machine. It includes diagrams showing correct and incorrect connector insertion, and setup instructions for DCEN Flux-Cored (gasless) welding polarity configuration.
visible_text: Loosen the Knob on the Wire Feed mechanism, then insert the Gun Cable Connector through the hole on the Welder front and into the socket on the Wire Feed
Ensure that the Gun Cable Connector is fully inserted into the socket on the Wire Feed mechanism as shown, then tighten the Knob securely
If Connector is not fully inserted, the gas connection will leak, preventing shielding gas from reaching the welding arc
NOTICE: To prevent damage, do not overtighten the Knob
Insert the Wire Feed Control Cable through the hole on the Welder front and connect it to the Wire Feed Control Socket inside the machine
DCEN Direct Current Electrode Negative Wire Setup for Flux-Cored (gasless) welding
Plug Ground Clamp Cable into Positive (+) Socket. Plug Wire Feed Power Cable into Negative (-) Socket
Incorrect—Connector not fully inserted
Correct—Connector fully inserted
Wire Feed Mechanism
Gun Cable Connector
Wire Feed Control Cable
Ground Clamp Cable in Positive Socket

title: DCEP Direct Current Electrode Positive Wire Setup
description: This manual page provides setup instructions for DCEP (Direct Current Electrode Positive) solid core gas shielded welding. It details the polarity configuration, cable connections, gas cylinder setup, and regulator installation for proper welding machine operation.
visible_text: DCEP Solid Core (Gas Shielded) Polarity Setup
Plug Ground Clamp Cable into Negative (-) Socket
Plug Wire Feed Power Cable into Positive (+) Socket
Ground Clamp Cable in Negative Socket
Wire Feed Power Cable in Positive Socket
Determine which type of shielding gas would be appropriate for the welding you will do
Set the cylinder onto a cabinet or cart near the Welder
Remove the cylinder's cap and briefly open valve to blow dust and dirt
Locate the Regulator and close its valve until it is loose, then thread Regulator onto cylinder
When using C100 shielding gas, connect the enclosed CGA 580/320 adapter

title: Wire Feeding and MIG Gun Assembly Instructions
description: This manual page provides detailed instructions for properly feeding welding wire into the wire inlet liner and feed guide, securing the wire with appropriate tension, and assembling the MIG gun components. It includes important safety warnings about maintaining wire tension to prevent tangling and feeding problems.
visible_text: Securely hold onto the end of the welding wire and keep tension on it during the following steps
Cut off all bent and crimped wire. The cut end must have no burrs or sharp edges
Keep tension on the wire and guide at least 12 inches of wire into the Wire Inlet Liner and Feed Guide
Make sure the welding wire is resting in the groove of the Feed Roller
The tension should be 3-5 for solid wire and 2-3 for flux-cored wire
Stainless steel wire is less flexible than other welding wire
Pull the Nozzle to remove it
Unscrew the Contact Tip counterclockwise and remove
Lay the MIG Gun Cable out in a straight line so that the welding wire moves through it easily
For technical questions, please call 1-800-444-3353

title: Welding Machine Safety and Setup Instructions
description: This manual page provides critical safety warnings about electric shock hazards from welding voltage, along with detailed setup instructions for power connection, trigger operation, and wire feeding procedures for a MIG welding gun.
visible_text: DANGER: PARTS MAY BE AT WELDING VOLTAGE TO PREVENT ELECTRIC SHOCK AND DEATH
Keep hands away from Wire Feed mechanism
Close door before plugging in, unless using Cold Wire Feed to feed wire through to gun
Do not touch Trigger while feeding wire through to gun
Plug either 120VAC or 240VAC Power Cord into Power Input Socket
Do not touch the Gun's Trigger. Plug the Power Cord into a properly grounded, GFCI protected 120VAC (20 amp rated) or 240VAC receptacle
Point the Gun away from all objects. Press and hold the Cold Wire Feed Switch until the wire feeds through two inches
The wire liner may come out with the welding wire. This is normal, just push the wire liner back into the Gun
If the wire does not feed properly and the Spool is stationary, turn OFF and unplug the Welder and slightly tighten the Feed Tensioner clockwise before retrying
Power Input
Power Switch
Cold Wire Feed Switch
MIG Gun
Welding Wire
Item 57812

title: Wire Drive Tension Check and MIG Gun Setup
description: This manual page provides instructions for checking welding wire drive tension and setting up the MIG gun with contact tips and nozzle. It also includes detailed steps for optional spool gun setup with cable connections and gas hose configuration.
visible_text: To check the wire's drive tension, press and hold Trigger to feed the wire against a piece of wood from 2 to 3 inches away
Incrementally increase tension until wire bends
Select a Contact Tip that is compatible with the welding wire used
Replace the Nozzle and cut the wire off at 1/2" from tip (1/2" stickout)
Optional Spool Gun Setup
To set up optional Spool Gun, plug Ground Clamp Cable into Negative Socket
Ensure Cable Connector is fully inserted and tighten the Knob securely
Connect Gas Hose on Spool Gun Cable Connector to Spool Gun Gas Outlet
Wire Feed Power Cable in Positive Socket
Ground Clamp Cable in Negative Socket

title: Basic Wire Welding Safety and Controls
description: This manual page covers safety precautions, protective equipment requirements, and basic information about different welding processes including flux-cored wire, MIG, and aluminum welding. It includes warnings about arc light damage, proper gear requirements, and instructions to practice welding techniques on scrap material before working on projects.
visible_text: Read the ENTIRE IMPORTANT SAFETY INFORMATION section
Protective gear must be worn when using the Welder
Minimum shade number 10 full face shield
Light from the arc can cause permanent damage to the eyes and skin
Do not breathe arc fumes
Flux-cored wire welding is used to weld mild steel and stainless steel without shielding gas
MIG welding uses solid wire and shielding gas
Aluminum welding can be performed with an optional Spool Gun
Good welding takes a degree of skill and experience
Make practice welds on pieces of scrap to practice technique before welding anything of value
Keep welding area clear of grounded objects whenever unit is plugged in and turned on
Power Switch
For technical questions, please call 1-800-444-3353

title: Duty Cycle and Weld Setup Instructions
description: This manual page explains duty cycle limitations for welding machines to prevent overheating, provides rated duty cycle specifications for 120VAC and 240VAC models, and instructs users on proper workpiece setup and ground cable clamping for welding.
visible_text: Duty Cycle (Duration of Use)
Rated Duty Cycle 120VAC - 40% Use at 100A - 4 Minutes Welding, 6 Minutes Resting
Rated Duty Cycle 240VAC - 25% Use at 200A - 2-1/2 Minutes Welding, 7-1/2 Minutes Resting
100% Continuous Use at 75A
100% Continuous Use at 115A
Setting Up The Weld
Make practice welds on pieces of scrap
Use clamps to hold the workpieces in position
Clamp Ground Cable to bare metal on the workpiece
Clean surfaces to bare metal
Chamfer thick workpieces
Internal thermal protection system
Notice: When welding equipment on a vehicle, disconnect the vehicle battery power

It includes detailed steps for adjusting wire diameter, material thickness, wire feed speed, and voltage through the control knobs and LCD display.
visible_text: Turn the Power Switch to the OFF position
GFCI protected 120VAC (20 amp rated) or 240VAC receptacle
Set MIG Gun down on nonconductive, nonflammable surface
Turn the Power Switch ON
MIG Stainless Steel
Set Wire Diameter and Material Thickness
Turn Left Knob to set wire diameter
Turn Right Knob to set material thickness

Turn Left Knob to adjust Wire Feed Speed (Amperage)
Turn Right Knob to adjust Voltage

Basic Wire Welding Technique and Gun Angles

It provides visual diagrams showing proper gun positioning and welding bead patterns.

stringer bead
weave bead
Weld MIG Gun angles, viewed from front of weld joint
fillet weld joint
butt weld joint
Push Angle 0-15°
Drag Angle 0-15°
CTWD - Contact Tip to Work Distance
Solid Wire with Shielding Gas
Flux-Cored Wire without Gas

45° angle for fillet welds

title: Welder Shutdown and Maintenance Procedures
description: This page provides post-welding shutdown procedures, duty cycle guidelines for different voltage settings, and maintenance instructions for MIG welding equipment. It includes safety warnings about hot welds and proper cooling procedures before unplugging.
visible_text: Allow the Welder to cool with the Power Switch on
FOLLOW DUTY CYCLE!
120VAC 40% Use at 100A For 10 Continuous Minutes
240VAC 25% Use at 200A For 10 Continuous Minutes
4 Minutes Welding 6 Minutes Resting
2-1/2 Minutes Welding 7-1/2 Minutes Resting
100% Continuous Use at 75A
100% Continuous Use at 115A
When welding is complete, set the MIG Gun down on a heat-proof surface
Remove Ground Clamp from workpiece or table
Respool wire by clipping wire, removing gas nozzle
Close shielding gas cylinder valve securely
CAUTION: Weld will be hot, do not touch

description: TIG setup diagram showing ground clamp cable in the positive socket and TIG torch cable in the negative socket.
title: TIG Polarity Setup
Connect Shielding Gas and Power Cord Setup
description: This manual page provides step-by-step instructions for connecting a 100% Argon shielding gas cylinder to a TIG welding machine and plugging in the power cord. It includes diagrams showing the gas cylinder setup, regulator connection, and power input specifications.
visible_text: Connect Shielding Gas
With assistance, place an 100% Argon cylinder onto a cabinet or cart near the Welder
Remove the cylinder's cap. Stand to the side of the valve opening, then open the valve briefly to blow dust and dirt from the valve opening.
Locate the Regulator (included) and close its valve until it is loose, then thread Regulator onto cylinder
Connect Shielding Gas Hose on TIG Torch Cable Connector to the Regulator's Outlet
Connect Power Cord
Plug either 120VAC or 240VAC Power Cord into Power Input Socket
Note: Plug will only fit one way.
WARNING! TO PREVENT SERIOUS INJURY FROM ACCIDENTAL OPERATION: Do not plug cord into wall outlet at this time.
Item 57812
Page 25

title: Sharpen Tungsten Electrode and Assemble TIG Torch
description: This manual page provides instructions for sharpening tungsten electrodes and assembling a TIG torch for welding. It includes safety warnings, step-by-step procedures with numbered instructions, and labeled diagrams showing the torch components and electrode grinding technique.
visible_text: Sharpen Tungsten Electrode (sold separately)
WARNING! TO PREVENT SERIOUS INJURY: Some Electrodes may have materials added to them that are hazardous to breathe
Dedicate a fine grit grinding wheel exclusively to Electrode grinding
Lightly press Electrode tip against the surface of the grinding wheel at an angle
The conical portion of the ideal tip will be 2-1/2 times as long as the Electrode diameter
Re-insert Electrode into Collet with tip protruding 1/8"-1/4" beyond the Ceramic Nozzle
Assemble TIG Torch (sold separately)
Consult Settings Chart, on top of Welder, to determine proper Tungsten Electrode size
Match Collet and Collet Body sizes to Tungsten Electrode size
Lock Electrode in place with Back Cap
Insert Tungsten Electrode into Collet on front of Torch
Grinding direction must be parallel to length of Electrode

description: Stick setup diagram showing ground clamp cable in the negative socket and electrode holder cable in the positive socket.
title: Stick Polarity Setup
Basic TIG/Stick Welding Safety and Controls
description: This manual page provides essential safety warnings and basic information for TIG and Stick welding processes. It covers protective equipment requirements, different welding types, practice recommendations, and welding tip safety precautions.
visible_text: Basic TIG/Stick Welding
Read the ENTIRE IMPORTANT SAFETY INFORMATION section
TO PREVENT SERIOUS INJURY: Protective gear must be worn when using the Welder
minimum shade number 10 full face shield
DC TIG Welding is used to weld mild steel and stainless steel using a TIG Rod and shielding gas
AC TIG Welding is used to weld aluminum using a TIG Rod and shielding gas
Stick Welding is used to weld mild steel and stainless steel using a Stick Electrode without shielding gas
Good welding takes a degree of skill and experience
Make practice welds on pieces of scrap to practice technique before welding anything of value
Keep welding tip clear of grounded objects whenever unit is plugged in and turned on
Practice your welding technique on scrap pieces before welding anything of value
For technical questions, please call 1-800-444-3353

Page 28

title: Duty Cycle and Weld Setup Instructions
description: This manual page explains duty cycle limitations for welding machines to prevent overheating and damage, provides rated duty cycle specifications for TIG and Stick welders, and gives step-by-step instructions for properly setting up a weld including workpiece preparation and ground cable connection.
visible_text: Duty Cycle (Duration of Use)
40% duty cycle at 125A weld allowed to rest for at least 6 minutes after every 4 minutes
TIG Rated Duty Cycles - 240VAC 30% Use at 175A, 120VAC 40% Use at 125A
Stick Rated Duty Cycles - 240VAC 25% Use at 175A, 120VAC 40% Use at 80A
100% Continuous Use at 105A (TIG 240VAC)
100% Continuous Use at 90A (TIG 120VAC)
Setting up the Weld
Make practice welds on pieces of scrap the same thickness
Clean the weld surfaces thoroughly with a wire brush or angle grinder
Use clamps (not included) to hold the workpieces in position
Clamp Ground Cable to bare metal on the workpiece
When welding equipment on a vehicle, disconnect the vehicle battery power
Item 57812
Page 29

title: TIG Welding Setup and Controls Instructions
description: This manual page provides detailed instructions for setting up and operating a TIG welding machine, including safety warnings, control panel layout, and step-by-step procedures for adjusting settings such as gas flow, polarity, rod diameter, and amperage.
visible_text: TIG Welding
WARNING: TO PREVENT SERIOUS INJURY AND DEATH: Do not weld without Grounding Clamp
DANGER: TO PREVENT DEATH FROM ASPHYXIATION: Do not open gas without proper ventilation
Open gas cylinder's valve all the way
Set Flow Gauge to SCFH value indicated on the Settings Chart
Turn the Power Switch to the OFF position
Press Home Button on Control Panel
Turn Main Control Knob until TIG process appears on LCD display screen
Set SCFH between 10-25
100% Argon
Turn Left Knob to set rod diameter
Turn Right Knob to set material thickness
Turn Left Knob to adjust output amperage
WARNING! Welder is now energized and Open Circuit Voltage is present
For technical questions, please call 1-800-444-3353

title: TIG Welding Operation and Safety Instructions
description: This manual page provides detailed instructions for operating a TIG welding machine, including torch handling, arc initiation, welding procedures, and post-operation shutdown steps. It emphasizes critical safety warnings regarding protective gear, proper grounding, and accident prevention measures.
visible_text: Hold TIG Torch in one gloved hand and the TIG Rod in other gloved hand
Maintain a constant distance between the Tungsten Electrode and the workpiece: between 1 and 1.5 times the diameter of the Electrode
Open valve on TIG Torch to start gas flow
Push Foot Pedal and touch Electrode to work piece and lift to initiate welding arc
When welding puddle is not enough, tilt Torch backward about 10-15 degrees from vertical
Remove the TIG Rod each time the Electrode is advanced, but do not remove it from the gas shield
Protective gear must be worn when using the Welder: minimum shade number 10 full face shield, ear protection, welding gloves, sleeves and apron, NIOSH-approved respirator
Metal work bench must be grounded when TIG welding
After practice welding on scrap, stop, and check your progress. Perform Strike Test according to Strike Test on page 34
Allow Welder to cool down after use

title: Stick Welding Setup and Operation Instructions
description: This manual page provides step-by-step instructions for setting up and operating a VULCAN Omni Pro 220 stick welder. It covers safety warnings, power setup, electrode selection, diameter and thickness settings, and auto weld settings with accompanying control panel diagrams.
visible_text: WARNING - TO PREVENT SERIOUS INJURY AND DEATH: Do not weld without Grounding Clamp
Turn the Power Switch to the OFF position
Plug the Welder into a properly grounded, GFCI protected, 120VAC or 240V outlet
Set Electrode Holder down on nonconductive, nonflammable surface
Turn Main Control Knob to set electrode type
Turn Left Knob to set electrode diameter
Turn Right Knob to set material thickness
Turn Left Knob to adjust output amperage
Turn Right Knob to ON to energize Electrode Holder
No Gas Required
VULCAN Omni Pro 220
Stick 7' Electrode 60xx
Auto Weld Settings: 60 A output amperage, 0.0 V

title: Welding Controls and Safety Instructions
description: This manual page covers optional settings for a stick welder including Hot Start, Arc Force, and Recall/Save Settings functions. It provides detailed instructions for igniting the arc, welding techniques, and critical safety warnings about protective equipment and arc hazards.
visible_text: Optional Settings
Hot Start - Adjust amperage at start of weld
Arc Force - Adjust weld penetration and smoothness
Recall Setting - Retrieve saved settings
Save Setting - Program up to 5 different configurations
WARNING! TO PREVENT SERIOUS INJURY
Protective gear must be worn including full face shield, ear protection, welding gloves, sleeves and apron
Place bare metal end of Stick Electrode inside jaws of Electrode Holder
Stroke the workpiece lightly to ignite the arc
Lift Electrode off workpiece the same distance as diameter of bare metal end
Tilt Electrode back 10 to 20 degrees
Turn the Power Switch OFF
Allow Welder to cool down
Clean, then store Welder and accessories indoors

title: Welding Tips, Strike Test, and Weld Cleaning
description: This manual page provides guidance on testing welding technique through the strike test method, which involves striking a practice weld with a hammer to evaluate weld quality. It also includes instructions on cleaning welds using proper safety equipment and tools such as chipping hammers and wire brushes.
visible_text: Welding Tips
Strike Test
A good way to test welding technique is to examine a weld's appearance after it has cooled
TIG welding is a complicated process, requiring experience and skill
GOOD WELD bends and is not brittle
POOR WELD snaps or cracks
Cleaning the Weld
Use a chipping hammer to knock off slag
Use a wire brush to further clean the weld
ANSI-approved safety goggles and protective wear when cleaning
For technical questions, please call 1-800-444-3353
Item 57812
Page 34

title: Wire Weld Diagnosis Workpiece Heat Control
description: This manual page provides diagnostic guidance for wire welding, showing how to identify inadequate, proper, and excess weld penetration based on heat control. It includes methods to adjust workpiece heat through current, travel speed, and wire feed settings, plus example weld diagrams with common issues and corrections.
visible_text: Wire Weld Diagnosis—Workpiece Heat Control / Weld Penetration
Inadequate Penetration
Proper Penetration
Excess Penetration or Burn-Through
Increase weld current
Decrease travel speed
Decrease weld current
Increase travel speed
Good Weld
Voltage Too Low or Wire Feed Too Slow
Voltage Too High or Wire Feed Too Fast
Travel Speed Too Fast
Travel Speed Too Slow
CTWD Too Long or Wrong Polarity
For technical questions, please call 1-800-444-3353

title: Wire Weld Penetration and Common Welding Defects
description: This manual page provides guidance on wire weld penetration control, identifying proper versus improper welds, and troubleshooting common welding defects including excess penetration, inadequate penetration, bend at joint, weld not adhering properly, and slag coating. It includes visual profile views and causes/solutions for each defect type.
visible_text: Wire Weld Penetration (Workpiece Heat Control)
EXCESS PENETRATION OR BURN-THROUGH
PROPER PENETRATION
INADEQUATE PENETRATION
Bend at Joint
Weld Not Adhering Properly
Coat of Slag Over Weld
Workpiece overheating: Reduce wire feed speed. Decrease weld current.
Incorrect welding technique: Maintain 1/2" or less CTWD.
Workpieces too thick/close: Bevel thick workpieces, allow slight gap, and weld in several passes.
Improper clamping: Clamp workpieces securely. Make tack welds to hold workpieces.
Excessive heat: Weld a small portion and allow to cool before proceeding.
Gas-shielded MIG welds are protected by the shielding gas and do not need slag to protect them.

description: Diagnosis page showing porosity examples and likely causes for wire welding.
title: Wire Weld Porosity Diagnosis
Stick Weld Diagnosis Workpiece Heat Control Penetration
description: This page provides guidance on diagnosing stick welds by examining weld penetration and heat control. It shows three penetration conditions (inadequate, proper, excess), control methods for adjusting heat, and visual examples of TIG stick welds with corresponding corrections for common defects.
visible_text: Stick Weld Diagnosis–Workpiece Heat Control / Weld Penetration
INADEQUATE PENETRATION
PROPER PENETRATION
EXCESS PENETRATION OR BURN-THROUGH
Not hot enough
Ideal heat
Too hot
How to increase workpiece heat and increase penetration
How to reduce workpiece heat and limit penetration
CLEAN WELDS FIRST!
Stick welds will have a coat of slag over them until cleaned
Good Weld
Current Too Low
Current Too High
Weld Speed Too Fast
Weld Speed Too Slow
Arc Length Too Short
Arc Length Too Long

title: Stick Weld Penetration and Defect Control
description: This manual page illustrates three common stick welding defects: penetration issues, weld adhesion problems, and bend at joint. For each defect, it provides profile view diagrams and lists possible causes with corresponding solutions for proper weld quality control.
visible_text: Stick Weld Penetration (Workpiece Heat Control)
EXCESS PENETRATION OR BURN-THROUGH
PROPER PENETRATION
INADEQUATE PENETRATION
Workpiece overheating: Reduce current
Welding speed too slow: Increase welding speed
Incorrect welding technique: Keep arc on leading edge of weld puddle
Insufficient weld heat: Increase current
Stick Weld–Weld Not Adhering Properly
Stick Weld–Bend at Joint
Improper clamping: Clamp workpieces securely
Excessive heat: Weld a small portion and allow to cool

title: Stick Weld Defects Causes and Solutions Guide
description: This manual page illustrates common stick welding defects including slag coating, porosity, crooked beads, excessive spatter, and burn-through. Each defect is shown with a top view diagram and lists possible causes with corresponding solutions for quality welding.
visible_text: Stick Weld–Coat of Slag Over Weld
Slag is a necessary part of a stick weld
Stick Weld–Porosity
Small cavities or holes in the bead
Stick Weld–Crooked/Wavy Bead
Stick Weld–Excessive Spatter
Stick Weld–Burn-Through
Base material melts away, leaving a hole in the weld
Dirty workpiece or fill material
Inconsistent welding speed
Workpiece overheating
Fine spatter is normal

title: Welding Machine Maintenance and MIG Gun Inspection
description: This maintenance page provides instructions for inspecting and cleaning the MIG gun nozzle and contact tip, as well as procedures for replacing the LCD screen cover. It includes safety warnings about allowing the welder to cool before service and outlines pre-use inspection requirements.
visible_text: TO PREVENT SERIOUS INJURY, FIRE AND BURNS: Unplug the Welder, rest the tool on a heat-proof, electrically non-conductive surface, and allow all parts of the Welder to cool thoroughly before service.
BEFORE EACH USE, inspect the general condition of the Welder
MIG Gun Nozzle and Contact Tip Inspection and Cleaning
Make sure that the entire MIG Gun is completely cool and that the Power Cord is unplugged from the electrical outlet before proceeding.
Scrub the interior of the Nozzle clean with a wire brush.
Unscrew the Contact Tip counterclockwise and slide it off the welding wire to remove.
Examine the shape of the hole at the end of the Contact Tip. It should be an even circle.
Replacing the LCD Screen Cover
Carefully pry frame off of Display (22) by inserting flathead screwdriver into one of the side slots.
Insert a new Screen Cover (17) into Screen Frame (18) with the gap facing downward.

description: Troubleshooting table for wire feed issues, unstable arc, weak arc strength, and related welding faults.
title: MIG and Flux-Cored Troubleshooting

description: Troubleshooting page that includes Porosity in the Weld Metal causes and remedies.
title: Porosity Troubleshooting
TIG/Stick Welding Troubleshooting Guide
description: This troubleshooting page provides common problems, possible causes, and solutions for TIG and Stick welding equipment issues. It covers problems such as welder non-function, LCD display failure, weak arc strength, and unstable welding arc with systematic diagnostic and repair guidance.
visible_text: Troubleshooting–TIG/Stick Welding
Be CERTAIN to shut off the Welder, disconnect it from power, and discharge the electrode to ground before adjusting, cleaning, or repairing the unit
Welder Does Not Function When Switched On
Reduce duration or frequency of welding periods
Attach Ground Clamp to workpiece
Connect shielding gas to Welder
LCD Display Does Not Light When Welder is Switched On
Verify the voltage at the outlet and the connection to the outlet
Check circuit breaker/GFCI devices
Weak Arc Strength
Check the line voltage
Do not use an extension cord on this Welder
Welding Arc Not Stable
Loose electrode cable or ground cable
Replace shielding gas cylinder
